expanded_description: A kite is a quadrilateral with two distinct pairs of consecutive congruent sides. Unlike parallelograms, kites generally have no parallel sides. Their unique structure produces distinctive diagonal and angle properties: the diagonals are perpendicular, one diagonal bisects the other, one pair of opposite angles is congruent, and one diagonal bisects a pair of opposite angles. Kites appear in design, origami, and engineering where symmetry along a single axis is needed.

core_concept.principle: A kite is a quadrilateral with two pairs of adjacent congruent sides. Key properties: (1) The diagonals are perpendicular. (2) Exactly one diagonal bisects the other—the longer diagonal cuts the shorter one in half. (3) Exactly one pair of opposite angles is congruent—the angles between the non-congruent sides. (4) One diagonal bisects a pair of opposite angles. The kite is symmetric about its longer diagonal.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a classic diamond-shaped kite. The top two sides are equal, and the bottom two sides are equal, but the top and bottom sides may differ. The long diagonal runs from top to bottom and is a line of symmetry—fold along it, and the two halves match perfectly. The short diagonal runs side to side and is perpendicular to the long one. The long diagonal cuts the short one exactly in half. The angles at the left and right points (where unequal sides meet) are equal. The diagonal that is the line of symmetry bisects the angles at the top and bottom. These properties make kites distinct from all other quadrilaterals.

image.alt: A kite with two pairs of adjacent congruent sides marked. Diagonals drawn—longer one as symmetry axis, shorter one perpendicular and bisected. Congruent opposite angles marked. Angle bisectors at symmetry axis vertices.
image.caption: Kite: perpendicular diagonals, one bisects the other, one pair of opposite angles ≅, symmetry diagonal bisects angles.

sections.section_title: Definition and Basic Properties of a Kite
sections.introduction: A kite is defined by two distinct pairs of adjacent congruent sides. This creates a quadrilateral with a single line of symmetry and distinctive diagonal and angle properties.

introduction_image.alt: A kite with vertices labeled. Two pairs of adjacent congruent sides marked: AB≅AD and CB≅CD.
introduction_image.caption: Kite: two pairs of consecutive congruent sides.

key_concept.definition: A kite is a quadrilateral with two pairs of consecutive congruent sides. If ABCD is a kite with AB ≅ AD and CB ≅ CD, then diagonal AC (connecting the vertices where congruent sides meet) is the symmetry axis. Diagonal BD is the cross diagonal. Four properties: (1) Diagonals are perpendicular (AC ⟂ BD). (2) The symmetry diagonal bisects the cross diagonal (AC bisects BD). (3) One pair of opposite angles is congruent—the angles between the non-congruent sides (∠B ≅ ∠D). (4) The symmetry diagonal bisects the angles at its endpoints (AC bisects ∠A and ∠C).
key_concept.context: These are proved using SSS congruence of the two triangles formed by the symmetry diagonal. CPCTC gives the angle properties and the bisector relationship. The area formula A = (1/2)d₁d₂ applies because the diagonals are perpendicular.

image.alt: Kite with diagonal AC dividing it into two congruent triangles. Perpendicular diagonals, bisected cross diagonal, congruent opposite angles, and angle bisectors labeled.
image.caption: Kite properties: proved via SSS congruence of the two triangles.

process_flow.process: Identify the Kite and Its Symmetry Diagonal
process_flow.description: Verify two pairs of adjacent congruent sides. The diagonal connecting the vertices where congruent sides meet is the symmetry axis.
process_flow.mechanism: Look for tick marks showing two pairs of equal adjacent sides. In kite ABCD with AB ≅ AD and CB ≅ CD, the symmetry diagonal is AC. The cross diagonal is BD.
process_flow.result: Symmetry diagonal and cross diagonal identified.
applications: 'Kite ABCD with AB ≅ AD and CB ≅ CD.' Symmetry diagonal = AC. Cross diagonal = BD.

image.alt: Kite with equal sides marked. Symmetry diagonal highlighted. Cross diagonal labeled.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify the symmetry diagonal and cross diagonal.

process_flow.description: Diagonals are perpendicular. The symmetry diagonal bisects the cross diagonal. Area = (1/2)(d₁)(d₂).
process_flow.mechanism: AC ⟂ BD. If diagonals intersect at E, then BE ≅ ED (cross diagonal is bisected). AC may or may not be bisected. The four triangles at the intersection are right triangles.
process_flow.result: Diagonal segment lengths or area found.

image.alt: Kite with perpendicular diagonals, right angles, bisected cross diagonal. Area formula displayed.
image.caption: Step 2: Diagonals perpendicular, cross diagonal bisected, area formula.

process_flow.description: One pair of opposite angles is congruent. The symmetry diagonal bisects angles at its endpoints.
process_flow.mechanism: ∠B ≅ ∠D (angles between non-congruent sides). ∠BAC ≅ ∠DAC and ∠BCA ≅ ∠DCA (symmetry diagonal bisects ∠A and ∠C). Sum of all angles = 360°.

image.alt: Kite with congruent opposite angles marked. Angle bisector marks at symmetry axis vertices.
image.caption: Step 3: One pair opposite angles ≅, symmetry diagonal bisects angles.

classifications.value: A concise reference for all kite properties.
characteristics: Diagonals: perpendicular. One bisects the other (symmetry bisects cross).
Angles: One pair of opposite angles ≅ (between non-congruent sides).
Symmetry diagonal: bisects angles at its endpoints. Is a line of reflection.
A rhombus is a special kite (all four sides equal). A square is a special rhombus and kite.

examples.explanation: $m\angle B = 95°$, $m\angle C = 60°$. $\angle B \cong \angle D$ → $m\angle D = 95°$. $m\angle A = 360° - 95° - 60° - 95° = 110°$.

image.caption: Using the 360° sum and congruent opposite angles.

image.alt: Complete kite section with all properties, area formula, and comparison to other quadrilaterals.
image.caption: Properties of Kites overview.

sections.introduction: Apply kite properties to solve problems and prove the key theorems.

worked_examples.title: Kite Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Kite ABCD: BD = 16. Find BE and ED where diagonals intersect at E.
2) Kite: m∠QPR = 40°. PR bisects ∠P. Find m∠P.
3) Kite: diagonals 12 and 18. Find area.
4) Kite: m∠B = 95°, m∠C = 60°. Find m∠A and m∠D.
5) Prove diagonals of a kite are perpendicular.

step_by_step.explanation: SSS: $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle ADC$. CPCTC: $\angle BAE \cong \angle DAE$. In isosceles $\triangle ABD$, the angle bisector from the vertex is also the perpendicular bisector of the base. So $AE \perp BD$. Thus diagonals are perpendicular.

image.caption: Kite problems: diagonal segments, angles, area, and proof.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) $BE = ED = 8$.
2) $m\angle P = 80°$.
3) Area $= 108$.
4) $m\angle A = 110°$, $m\angle D = 95°$.
5) See proof.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Kite diagonal and angle properties, area formula.

summary: A kite has two pairs of adjacent congruent sides. Diagonals are perpendicular; symmetry diagonal bisects the cross diagonal. One pair of opposite angles is congruent. Symmetry diagonal bisects angles at its endpoints. Area = (1/2)(d₁)(d₂). A rhombus and square are special kites.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with Kite in center, branches for diagonal properties, angle properties, area, and relationship to other quadrilaterals.
review_questions_image.caption: Properties of Kites Review.
